mnemonics.title: AAP - Always Analyze Potential
mnemonics.content: AAP stands for Amortized Analysis + Potential method. Remember: In Potential method, we store 'potential energy' in the data structure to pay for expensive future operations.

mnemonics.title: A Big Costly Sequence Needs Credit
mnemonics.content: ABC = Aggregate, Binary (Counter), Credit (Accounting Method). Use aggregate analysis for simple cases, accounting method when you need to track credits explicitly.

mnemonics.title: Dynamic Arrays Double Or Nothing
mnemonics.content: When a dynamic array (vector) is full, it doubles in size. Most insertions are O(1), but the rare expensive copy makes it O(2) amortized - doubling spreads the cost.

mnemonics.title: Stack MultiPop Pays Back
mnemonics.content: Push costs 1 credit. MultiPop 'pays back' using credits stored from previous pushes. Each pop costs 1, but if stack is full, MultiPop recovers multiple credits.

mnemonics.title: Counter Bits Flip, Cost Climbs
mnemonics.content: Binary counter increment: Most bits stay 0→1 (cheap), but 1→0 triggers cascade (expensive). Amortized cost = 2, not O(k) where k = bits.

mnemonics.title: Potential = Energy Stored
mnemonics.content: Φ(Di) = Potential after i operations. amortized cost = actual cost + ΔΦ = actual cost + (Φ_new - Φ_old). Cheap operations increase potential, expensive ones decrease it.

acronyms.usage: When analyzing algorithms where expensive operations are rare, average the cost over many operations.
Sum total cost of n operations, divide by n. Simplest method - no per-operation cost tracking needed.
Assign 'credits' to operations. Cheap operations deposit credits, expensive ones withdraw. Total credits must never go negative.
Define potential function Φ(D) representing stored energy. amortized cost = actual cost + ΔΦ. More general than accounting method.
Φ(D) measures 'energy' in data structure state D. Choose Φ carefully to prove desired amortized bounds.
T(n) = Σ(actual cost of operations). For amortized O(1), T(n) ≤ cn for some constant c.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a bar chart: X-axis = operation number (1 to n), Y-axis = actual cost. Draw a horizontal line at height 'amortized cost'. The area under bars equals total cost; this area should fit under rectangle of height amortized × n.
Imagine a bank account. Push = deposit $1 credit. Pop = withdraw $1. MultiPop = withdraw k credits. Keep balance ≥ 0 always. The 'balance' is your amortized savings.
Think of a water tank. Operations add or remove water. Cheap operations add water (increase Φ), expensive operations drain water (decrease Φ). The water level is your potential.
Draw stairs: Each step = array capacity (1,2,4,8,16...). Insertions are flat steps. At capacity boundary, jump up a big step (copy cost). The 'jump' gets rarer as array grows.
Think of a light board. 0000 → 0001 costs 1 flip. 0111 → 1000 costs 4 flips (cascade). Most increments flip 1 bit, rare ones flip many. Average = 2 flips.
Stack = cafeteria tray dispenser. Each Push loads a tray (costs 1, stores 1 credit). MultiPop unloads many trays at once, using stored credits. Most of the time, stack has credits waiting.

analogies.analogy: Toll booth on highway: Most cars pay $1 (cheap), but every 1000th car needs to widen the road (expensive $1000). Over 1000 cars, average = $2/car. The toll collects extra from cheap cars to pay for road widening.

Laundromat tokens: You buy tokens cheap ($1 each) throughout the month. When you have a big laundry day, you use accumulated tokens instead of paying full price. Your 'token balance' is the credit stored.

Battery charging: Your phone charges slowly when battery is low (cheap operation adds potential). When you use power-hungry apps (expensive operation), battery drains. The charge level is your potential function.

Moving to a bigger house: You move small items yourself (cheap insertions). When you run out of room, you hire movers (expensive resize). But you only move every 2x growth, so moving costs spread over many small moves.
Netflix subscription: You pay monthly (Push = cheap). When you watch 10 shows in one weekend (MultiPop = expensive), you're using credits from all those months of subscription fees you already paid.

Odometer in car: Going from 1999→2000 requires flipping all four digits (expensive). Going from 2000→2001 flips only last digit (cheap). Over 10,000 miles, most increments flip 1 digit, some flip all 4. Average = 2 digit flips.
